conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me review the official eligibility criteria to make sure I provide you with accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me start by asking you some questions to assess your eligibility.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. I have one child - my son Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Now I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. 

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

Are you responsible for Alex - meaning you live with him and provide for his care (such as food, clothes, and other needs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. He lives with me and I provide for all his needs - food, clothes, all of that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or are there any other unusual circumstances regarding his living arrangements that I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in care for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is important for determining your eligibility.

Can you tell me how long exactly Alex has been in care? And does he spend any regular time at home with you - for example, does he come home for visits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for about 7 weeks. And no, he doesn't come home regularly - he's there full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful.

Just to clarify - since Alex has been in care for 7 weeks (which is less than 8 weeks), and you mentioned he doesn't come home regularly, let me ask: Are you making any financial contributions toward Alex's upkeep while he's in care? This could include things like buying him clothes, presents, food, pocket money, or contributing financially to his accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to contribute financially towards his needs while he's been in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official rules, I can now provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news - you are currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Since Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, you are still within the 8-week period during which Child Benefit continues. This means you can claim Child Benefit for him now.

**However, please be aware:** After Alex has been in care for 8 weeks (which will be in about 1 week's time), your eligibility will end unless one of these exceptions applies:

- Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you, OR
- You regularly spend money on Alex (for things like clothes, presents, food, or pocket money)

If your circumstances change - for example, if Alex starts coming home for visits or if you begin contributing financially to his upkeep - you should contact HMRC as this could affect your continued eligibility after the 8-week period.

I hope this information is helpful. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and the claimant lives in the UK. According to the rules, when a child goes into care, Child Benefit payments continue for 8 weeks. Alex has been in care for 7 weeks, which is still within the 8-week period. Therefore, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time. However, it's important to note that once the 8-week period expires (in approximately 1 more week), eligibility will end unless Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or the claimant regularly spends money on him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in care for 7 weeks. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a child goes into care, payments continue for 8 weeks. Since Alex has only been in care for 7 weeks, the claimant is still eligible for Child Benefit. However, eligibility will end after the 8-week period unless specific exceptions apply (such as the child spending at least 24 hours per week at home or the claimant regularly contributing financially to the child's upkeep).
